skill.name: rust-2024-migration
skill.description: Guides Rust projects through Rust 2024 migration opportunities, including let chains, async closures, native async traits, match ergonomics, and gen blocks.
skill.summary: Review Rust code and suggest practical Rust 2024 edition migrations.
skill.author: EmilLindfors

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command patterns, a SQLite reference, and weak cryptography markers, but review shows they are false positives from fenced documentation examples in SKILL.md. The only actionable concern is a documented `cargo fix --edition` command, which is legitimate Rust migration guidance but should be run only in a trusted project with version control.

low_findings.title: Benign External Command Example
low_findings.description: SKILL.md includes `cargo fix --edition` as a Rust migration checklist step. This is not executed by the skill and the allowed tools only list Read and Grep, but users should run it only in trusted repositories with clean version control.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command appears inside a fenced bash documentation block and is a standard Rust migration command. The skill metadata does not grant shell execution tools, so the issue is guidance risk rather than executable behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Rust Examples Treated as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported many Ruby or shell backtick execution findings across fenced Rust and text examples. Review found no backtick shell execution semantics in those examples and no instruction to execute arbitrary user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown code fences containing Rust examples. They are educational snippets, not executable skill logic or shell interpolation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Marker
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography finding points to the frontmatter description on line 3. That line describes Rust 2024 migration topics and contains no cryptographic API, hash algorithm, or security-sensitive implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is plain metadata text. No weak cryptographic algorithm is present at that location.

low_findings.title: False Positive: SQLite Database File
low_findings.description: The SQLite database finding points to a Rust example call `self.db.query(id).await`. It is not a database file, credential store, or embedded data artifact.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is a single illustrative Rust method call in a fenced code block. No SQLite file path or database contents are present.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Run Cargo Migration Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends `cargo fix --edition` as a manual migration step. This command modifies project files, so users should review diffs after running it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is clearly present and can change files when a user runs it. It is still a standard Rust tool and is not automatically executed by the skill.

content.value_statement: Rust 2024 migration can be hard to scope across async code, control flow, and edition settings. This skill identifies practical refactors and explains when each Rust 2024 pattern is appropriate.
actual_capabilities: Identifies nested if-let and match patterns that can become Rust 2024 let chains.
Suggests native async closure syntax for code that uses manual cloning around async blocks.
Explains when native async functions in traits can replace async-trait usage.
Highlights match ergonomics changes that affect Rust 2024 pattern matching.
Provides a migration checklist for edition settings and cargo fix usage.
Suggests gen blocks for simple manual iterator implementations.
limitations: Does not run Cargo, compile code, or apply migrations automatically.
Requires users to verify Rust compiler versions and crate compatibility.
Does not replace a full project test suite or edition migration review.
Some examples rely on features that may require specific stable compiler versions.

use_cases.title: Prepare an Application for Rust 2024
use_cases.description: Review application code for edition settings, nested control flow, async closure patterns, and migration checklist items.

use_cases.title: Modernize Library APIs
use_cases.description: Evaluate whether library traits can use native async functions and where async-trait should remain for dynamic dispatch.

use_cases.title: Review Pull Requests for Edition Changes
use_cases.description: Check proposed Rust 2024 refactors for readability, feature suitability, and cases that still need manual validation.

prompt_templates.title: Find Simple Migration Opportunities
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small Rust file
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Rust code for Rust 2024 migration opportunities. Focus on let chains, async closures, and simple readability improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an Edition Upgrade
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Rust 2024 migration plan for this project. Include Cargo.toml changes, compiler version requirements, cargo fix usage, and manual review steps.

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate Async Trait Usage
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Rust traits and implementations. Explain which async-trait usages can move to native async fn and which should stay unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Complex Refactors
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Rust 2024 migration diff. Check semantic changes, match ergonomics, async behavior, iterator replacements, and test gaps.

output_examples.input: A Rust function with three nested option checks and an active flag check.
output_examples.output: The response recommends a let chain, explains the required Rust edition and compiler version, and notes the logic should remain unchanged.

output_examples.input: A repository trait using async-trait without dynamic dispatch.
output_examples.output: The response suggests native async functions in the trait, explains why async-trait may be unnecessary, and calls out compatibility checks.

output_examples.input: A manual iterator implementation that yields a numeric range.
output_examples.output: The response proposes a gen block version and advises testing iterator behavior before merging the refactor.

best_practices: Run migration commands on a clean branch and review every generated diff.
Confirm the minimum supported Rust version before adopting Rust 2024 features.
Keep async-trait when dynamic dispatch or object safety requirements still need it.
anti_patterns: Do not change the edition without running the test suite and reviewing compiler warnings.
Do not replace async-trait blindly when traits are used as trait objects.
Do not refactor nested control flow when the new let chain makes ownership less clear.

faq.question: Does this skill modify my Rust project automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides review guidance and examples. You decide which changes to apply.

faq.question: Can it run cargo fix for me?
faq.answer: No. The skill only documents cargo fix as a migration step. Run commands yourself in a trusted project.

faq.question: Which Rust edition does it target?
faq.answer: It targets Rust 2024 migration patterns and explains related compiler version requirements from the skill documentation.

faq.question: Can it remove async-trait everywhere?
faq.answer: No. It explains where native async traits may work and where async-trait may still be needed.

faq.question: Is it useful for code review?
faq.answer: Yes. It can help reviewers evaluate Rust 2024 refactors, migration risks, and missing validation steps.

faq.question: Does it guarantee Rust 2024 compatibility?
faq.answer: No. You still need compilation, tests, dependency checks, and manual review for your project.
